channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: AI poses 'Hiroshima'-style threat to humanity without global rules, says Cooper
item.description:
Artificial intelligence poses a “Hiroshima”-style risk to humanity if governments do not agree to curb how it is developed, the foreign secretary has warned.

Yvette Cooper urged countries, including the US and China, to agree international rules for AI, telling the Guardian she believes the issue will dominate foreign policy over the next two years.

In an essay covering her thoughts on everything from emerging technology to Palestine, Cooper said the world was at a dangerous moment, not least because of what she sees as the permanent withdrawal of the US from its role as a global arbiter.

And in a separate interview with the Guardian she spelled out her concerns over AI and the Palestine peace process in particular.

In her piece being published on Monday by the Chatham House thinktank, the foreign secretary said: “On nuclear, international agreement came only after the world saw the terrifying power of the new technology at Hiroshima – and asked what would happen if it fell into the wrong hands. We cannot afford to wait for an AI equivalent of Hiroshima before we act.”

She told the Guardian: “Across the world, people are feeling the same thing – there is amazing potential here, but there is also huge risk. We are already in a world where we have malign actors who will use technology against us – whether that be hybrid threats, whether that be state-backed criminal groups or other kinds of organisations, or extremists and terror groups.”

She added: “I think AI is going to end up being the dominant foreign policy issue that we deal with over the next two years.”

Cooper identified AI as just one area threatening global security at the moment as she also warned about the impact of the climate crisis, irregular migration and foreign interference on western liberal democracy.

Her essay gives one of the clearest and most comprehensive pictures of the foreign secretary’s worldview and where she thinks her department should devote its attention in the coming years.

It comes as senior Labour figures jostle for position in a likely Andy Burnham cabinet, with her former colleague David Miliband being tipped for a return to the Foreign Office in her place. Miliband will share his own thoughts on foreign policy in a lecture on Thursday entitled “Power and its missing guardrails”.

Cooper writes in her essay that European powers need to adjust to the idea that the US will no longer guarantee international peace and democracy, even after the end of Donald Trump’s presidency.

“We should no longer expect the US to play the role it once did,” she says. “There will continue to be issues where we disagree. But reduced dependence on any single ally will make us stronger.”

One answer, she says in her essay, is for the UK and EU to negotiate a more permanent settlement instead of trying constantly to renegotiate individual elements of their trading arrangements.

The government is putting the finishing touches on the latest round of EU renegotiation as ministers look to strike new deals on agriculture and electricity trading as well as visas for young people.

They were due to be announced at a summit later this month, but that has been pushed back as EU officials hope to engage with the incoming Burnham government first.

Cooper argues in her essay: “We need to develop a new, structured relationship with Europe, leading the development of its new security architecture, with a more European Nato at its core. And we must settle our relationship with the EU as a closer but stable partnership, rather than one based on endless incremental bargaining.”

She did not say, however, what shape that more stable partnership should take. Burnham told the Guardian last year he wanted the UK to rejoin the bloc, but has said more recently he would not seek to pursue that if he becomes prime minister.

And she warns that with Trump preoccupied with the war in Iran and the world turning its attention to other regions, countries were at risk of forgetting about delivering peace in Palestine.

She told the Guardian: “You have the 20-point plan … [but] my big worry is that it is running into the ground.”
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Heatwave forces France to reflect on cultural priorities - RFI
item.description:
One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site.

Heatwave forces France to reflect on cultural priorities

Issued on: Modified:

The recent heatwave in France, which saw record-breaking temperatures, is part of a long-term trend of global warming. Antoine Poincaré, a climate risk expert and head of Apave Climate School, says air conditioning alone is not the solution. France will have to learn to adapt and make some difficult choices.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate project aims to assess wildfire, drought and flood risks for Kamloops, Tk'emlups
item.description:
Climate project aims to assess wildfire, drought and flood risks for Kamloops, Tk'emlups

City, band to assess risks

The City of Kamloops and Tk’emlúps te Secwépemc are teaming up on a proposed climate-readiness project to assess shared risks and improve emergency preparedness.

“This work will address both local and our shared regional risks,” said Ty Helgason, the city's preparedness manager.

Helgason said the joint climate readiness initiative is aimed at strengthening climate adaptation capacity, reducing disaster risk, and improving community resilience.

He was joined by a number of Tk’emlúps te Secwépemc representatives on June 23 when he sought council's sign off on an application for federal grant funding.

He said the city and the band have already felt climate impacts, including more severe wildfire seasons, smoke, warmer temperatures, prolonged drought, flooding and changing streamflows.

A city staff report said B.C.'s air quality health index, which is measured on a 1-10 scale, was set at 7 or higher — indicating high risk — for 128 days between 2015 and 2025.

That’s a jump from the 14 days of high-risk air quality experienced between 2005 and 2014.

“These trends reinforce the need for coordinated long-term adaptation planning, rather than just responding when an incident happens,” Helgason said.

According to a city staff report, the project will cost $150,000. The City of Kamloops and the band are seeking the maximum grant contribution of $105,000, and the remaining amount would be provided through shared staff contributions, about $22,500 for each party.

The grant funding is being offered by the Federation of Canadian Municipalities’ Local Leadership for Climate Adaptation initiative.

City staff said they are not looking for any additional taxation funding for this application.

The project will include Indigenous knowledge, local experience and technical climate risk assessments, and is intended to identify practical actions to improve disaster mitigation, resilience and emergency preparedness.

Kamloops council voted unanimously to authorize the grant application, and the city’s participation as a co-lead partner with TteS.

"These are the types of collaborations and partnerships that are vital for both sides of the river,” said Coun. Bill Sarai.

More Kamloops News

  • Taking aim at puncturevineOliver - 12:00 pm
  • Artists wanted for galleryVernon - 12:00 pm
  • RIH family room turns oneKamloops - 12:00 pm
  • Evolution of our thinkingWriter's Bloc - 11:00 am
  • 'Significant' growth on fireBoston Bar - 10:40 am

More Top Stories >

item.title: Climate Change Is Already at the Bedside. Why Aren't We Learning About It?
item.description:
The World Health Organization has identified climate change as the single greatest threat to global health in the 21st century. This crisis spans borders and political sentiment, and increasingly, physicians will be expected to provide climate-informed care.

Surveys of healthcare professionals have shown that we, as medical trainees, are generally aware of this but lack the tools and frameworks needed to apply climate science to clinical care. Many future physicians want to learn more about planetary health, but there are currently no established universal planetary health standards for graduating physicians or other healthcare professionals in the U.S.

To build a climate-resilient healthcare workforce, we must urgently scale planetary health education in residency training. This means not siloing planetary health into an optional lecture series or a passion project for a few motivated trainees. It needs to be woven into the core fabric of residency training, aligned with competency frameworks, quality improvement curricula, and existing assessment structures, so that every resident learns to recognize climate-sensitive conditions, practice sustainable high-value care, and advocate for resilient health systems.

The Current Landscape in GME Planetary Health Education

Evidence consistently highlights that, despite calls from major medical societies, graduate medical education (GME) has been slow to adopt climate and health content. A needs assessment of internal medicine residents at one program found a mean confidence score of only 2.2 out of 5 across environmental health topics, reflecting a knowledge gap that extends to faculty.

Leveraging the success of climate education integration in medical school curricula, we must include planetary health in GME so that residents are equipped to translate theory into real-world practice that safeguards patient care and bolsters health system resilience.

The Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education (ACGME) should embed planetary health into its Common Program Requirements, utilizing established, targeted frameworks like the Planetary Health Report Card (PHRC) to mandate active, case-based clinical learning to move from isolated climate sessions to longitudinal, skills-based planetary health education that is treated as essential for safe, modern patient care.

A promising precedent for this integration is the ACGME-approved revision to Emergency Medicine GME program requirements, effective July 1, 2028. Emergency medicine residents will be required to demonstrate competence in recognizing and managing the health impacts of climate change, while also providing evidence-based guidance on prevention and risk mitigation. This offers a blueprint for the rest of GME.

Implementation of GME Planetary Health Education

Experiential learning has been shown to be an appropriate method of education for skills and scenarios that can be difficult to explore in a traditional classroom setting. This can be complemented by integrating climate and health into routine aspects of training, including small-group case discussions, core lecture content, bedside teaching on climate-sensitive conditions, quality improvement projects, and longitudinal curricula woven through existing rotations.

These approaches offer a bridge from abstract planetary health concepts to the day-to-day clinical reasoning, communication, and systems-based practice skills residents need to address the emerging realities of climate and health. While the push for climate and health education in residency programs has largely been adopted within lecture materials, some programs have also started to introduce interactive, team-based activities. As an adjunct to didactics, these workshops encouraged residents to assess planetary health from a quality-improvement lens, a growing approach to integrating sustainability into healthcare systems. Resident feedback was positive.

The PHRC serves as the ideal self-assessment tool to facilitate this process, currently offering undergraduate medical education-specific, data-driven curricula benchmarks that can be easily adapted to GME criteria. It also recognizes themes of climate resilience as a relevant area of interest to be assessed within residency training as hospital dynamics and infrastructure become a concern in the face of worsening climate conditions.

The implementation of a GME-specific report card will allow for a more cohesive, cross-disciplinary assessment of this work and how best to incorporate experiential learning models. In fact, we're already starting to see this at medical schools across the country. This ultimately supports the evidence that medical schools and GME alike are seeking opportunities to address and propose solutions to the climate and health crisis.

Challenges for Planetary Health Education

Some may argue that residency programs are already stretched by intensive clinical hours and other educational mandates. Programs struggle to find space without displacing traditional biomedical content and cite barriers including a lack of faculty expertise and limited resources. However, leaving planetary health out will lead to patchy, inequitable adoption. Integrating this material does not require a disruptive overhaul; rather, it can be woven into existing structures, like resident-led quality improvement projects and case didactics.

Others may contend that achieving universal buy-in across complex, hospital-affiliated systems is difficult given institutional inertia. Yet, environmental determinants of health are inextricably linked to the socio-structural determinants we see daily. Because climate change disproportionately harms marginalized communities, climate competence is fundamentally an issue of health equity and environmental justice.

While this movement in medical schools has traditionally relied on "bottom-up" pressure from enthusiastic medical students, formal ACGME accreditation requirements are necessary to protect standards against shifting political landscapes and ensure system-wide institutional accountability. Residents can help mobilize this effort by signing onto formal letters, driving local reform with sustainability quality improvement projects, and engaging national networks to create grassroots coalitions to scale advocacy efforts.

From Individual Advocacy to Institutional Change

Incorporating planetary health into GME curricula is critical to empower future physicians to face climate change, the most pressing health threat worldwide. Residents would benefit from hands-on learning experiences to strengthen their existing clinical knowledge. The ACGME must act to mandate planetary health in residency training, ensuring that the next generation of physicians is fully prepared to practice medicine on a changing planet.

The authors' views are their own and do not necessarily reflect those of their institutions.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: 1602 Severe Weather/Event Reports FRI/SAT; More Today and Tomorrow Focused on Mid-Atlantic
item.description:
Another Regenerating Europe Heatwave Ahead
07/01/2026, 1:08 pm EDT

Climate Impact Company U.S. Sunday Report

Issued: Sunday, July 5, 2026

Highlight: 1, 602 severe weather/event reports FRI/SAT. More Mid-Atlantic severe storms today; Excessive rainfall event Northeast U.S. extends to Tuesday.

Fig. 1-2: NOAA/SPC severe weather/event reports for Friday and Saturday focused on NYC (Friday) and Washington/Baltimore/Philadelphia (Saturday).

Discussion: A cold front pushing into extreme heat was the catalyst for exceptionally violent severe weather outbreaks extending from the Ohio Valley to New York City on Friday (Fig. 1) and throughout the Mid-Atlantic region on Saturday (Fig. 2). NOAA/SPC tallied 1,602 severe weather events/reports for the 2 days.

NOAA/SPC severe weather outlooks for today regenerate significant risk of damaging wind in the DCA/BWI/PHL stretch again today (Fig. 3). Additionally, the stalled front triggering the adverse weather causes excessive rain and flood risk across Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Maryland, and Delaware today (Fig. 4). A repeat of excessive rainfall and flooding extends throughout the coastal Mid-Atlantic region on Monday (Fig. 5). NBM projects the most rainfall in the NYC area plus Connecticut through the next several days (Fig. 6).

The East U.S. heat weakens this week although staying hot in the Carolinas and likely to continue through the medium range. However, the heat core shifts westward to the Interior West in the 6-10-day period (Fig. 7) and expands/intensifies from California to the Great Plains in the 11-15-day period (Fig. 8). Note that the Costal Northwest and Northeast States avoid the regenerating anomalous heat.

U.S. population weight CDD forecasts stay hot over the weekend and are revised slightly hotter than yesterday through the middle third of July (Fig. 9). The ECM Operational (model) is hottest. The short-term CDD forecast is maintained in the week 3-5 outlook by ECM while CFS V2 and 3 AI models cool the northeast quadrant of the U.S. causing near normal CDD forecasts (Fig. 10). Mega-cluster ensemble favors the warmer scenario entering the 16-30-day period (47% confidence while 3 lesser likely ensembles support the cooler forecast.

Fig. 3-6: NOAA/SPC significant risk of damaging wind gusts in the DCA/BWI/PHL stretch today plus NOAA/WPC excessive rainfall/flood risk areas for today and tomorrow. Also, the NBM 3-day rainfall amount forecast.

Fig. 7-8: GFS+ECM U.S. medium range temperature anomaly forecast.

Fig. 9: The U.S. population weight CDD forecast utilizing all operational models, their consensus, and comparing with 24 hours ago and the 30-year/10-year normal.

Fig. 10: Estimated U.S. population weight CDD for weeks 3-5 ahead utilizing ECM, CFS V2, and 3 AI models.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Could abandoned energy pipelines help bring new water west to Arizona?
item.description:
Could abandoned energy pipelines help bring new water west to Arizona?

Joan Meiners

  • As the growing and drought-stricken West searchers for more water, one idea is to repurpose millions of miles of existing, unused energy pipelines.
  • Experts note that converting these pipelines could bypass legal and construction hurdles associated with building new ones.
  • But significant challenges include the high cost of cleaning the pipes and the risk of water contamination.

This is part of a monthly series answering readers' climate-related questions and highlighting Arizona's climate leaders. The stories aim to help connect and inspire Arizonans who care about protecting a livable climate and may be struggling to find hope in that effort. You can ask a question or nominate an Arizona climate leader by filling out this form at https://forms.gle/isK4e3Ym5KnS4dgE8 or by emailing climate reporter Joan Meiners at joan.meiners@arizonarepublic.com.

Environmentalists love a good recycling project. But does that make recycling energy infrastructure to transport water from states enduring flooding to those weathering drought a good idea?

Arizonans are ever on the hunt for a creative solution to the state's water woes. Legions of lawyers and legislators log thousands of billable hours arguing with other states for a larger piece of the Colorado River pie. Squads of scientists spend years searching for hidden springs. Meanwhile, climate change extracts its own cut.

As the countdown to a Colorado River deal ticks on a seemingly broken watch and wildfires rage across an American Southwest made more flammable by higher temperatures and parched soils, ideas trickle in, and dry up.

It's all pretty soul-dessicating (except, maybe, for some of the more promising results unearthed by an Arizona-based team of hydrologists in their report last month on how the state could capture and store more of its rainfall). The lawyers and legislators have inspired little hope.

This water gridlock might explain why more engineering-minded or big-horizon dreamer types invariably circle back on an idea that others have tried to flush — importing excess water from areas where it's not wanted to places, like Arizona, where it desperately is.

In May, The Arizona Republic addressed an inquiry of exactly this nature from a reader, who asked whether state leaders had considered "develop(ing) a type of channel or aqueduct or pipeline to move water (from flooding zones) to areas in AZ facing water shortages."

"Sure it would be hard, but ancient civilizations did it and other countries are doing it now," Phoenix resident and Arizona Republic reader Lisa Nelson wrote.

She's not wrong. Ancient Rome is famous for its aqueducts, which fueled its expansion into an empire far beyond its hydrologic means. And China is now in the midst of constructing a massive South-North Water Transfer Project to balance out water availability across its environments.

But in the United States in modern times, the complex legal and logistical process of clearing right-of-way access and then launching a major infrastructure project across half a dozen states between the Mississippi and Colorado Rivers would be prohibitively expensive and time-consuming, if not impossible, experts told The Republic in May.

That's when Tom Holley, a real estate broker based in Lake Havasu City, weighed in — to point out that a vast network of unused energy pipelines crisscrossing the country already exists. (Submit your question anonymously or otherwise here: https://forms.gle/isK4e3Ym5KnS4dgE8)

Holley wrote about a veritable fishnet of abandoned pipelines from California to New York and from Canada to Mexico that once carried natural gas, coal slurry or hazardous liquids back and forth beneath Americans' feet. Charted out by the National Pipeline Mapping System, these already-built pipes (many of which are still in use, but feasible to repurpose in the future) represent more than 3 million miles of possibility, he suggested.

The July climate reader question thus became: "Would active or inactive coal slurry lines that already span across the United States be an option?"

Wouldn't that be a happy human hack — if the answer to Western water wars, one of climate change's most complex and conflict-laden challenges, literally lay within the fossil fuel pipelines that once delivered us to this problem?

The idea has merit. It's even environmentally-friendly in the sense that it would limit additional, unnecessary land disturbance and use of mined resources by recycling existing pipelines for a new-age purpose.

It also has some precedent, sort of.

In 2022, engineers contracted by Cadiz Inc. — which bills itself as a "water solutions provider" but has been tied to mining interests and a host of corporate misdeeds — concluded that a 220-mile pipeline once used to carry natural gas for El Paso Natural Gas, a subsidiary of Kinder Morgan Inc., could be refashioned to move pumped groundwater from the Mojave aquifer to urban water distribution systems across Southern California.

The conversion project was never completed, mostly due to Cadiz's financial fumbles and the environment-related objections from opponents too numerous to name but that include local tribes and California Gov. Gavin Newsom.

But Cadiz Inc. has stood by its engineering design and the assertion that using old energy pipelines to move water to where people need it could be done, even as prospects of the scheme ever being uncorked continue to dry up.

Their commitment to the concept, others argue, may have more to do with the other main thing the Cadiz Water Project has going for it — the same advantage that prompted reader Holley's question — that using an existing pipeline could bypass the expensive, legally tricky and time consuming right of way authorizations required to build a new one. From there, swapping permits for which liquid it transports would be more straightforward paperwork.

"It would not be difficult, especially in this regulatory climate, to get a manner of use change from energy to water supply," said Kyle Roerink, executive director of the Great Basin Water Network, an organization that promotes water conservation in the West and has opposed several water pipeline projects. "That is part of why the location for Cadiz, which (environmentalists) have been fighting for years, is so palatable for exportation interests. There is a history of conveyance of resources that are in demand."

But that's about where the feasibility of reusing national gas and coal slurry lines to move water long distances ends, said Roerink, who also weighed in on Lisa Nelson's reader question about building a new water transfer system.

"I would say that the pipelines from the Navajo or Mojave plants would not be suitable for anything other than the right of ways," he said, referencing two decommissioned coal-fired power plants in the region. "The logical fallacies certainly begin with the fact that there is no excess water anywhere." (He explained how even floodwaters serve an ecological purpose in his response to the question from May.)

"It is a dangerous equivocation, in my opinion," Roerink said, "to compare the conveyance of energy to the distribution of culinary water."

Aging infrastructure would require expensive remediation to ensure against toxicity and leakage. After digging up all the pipes to clean or line them, you'd have given up most of the environmental and cost advantage, he said. And since this hasn't been done at scale, you still couldn't be sure the water would be safe to drink.

"The idea of re-using the pipe is like re-using a needle. Maybe you can get away with it. But there are risks."

From an academic and logistical standpoint, water infrastructure expert and Arizona State University sustainable engineering professor Margaret Garcia agrees — though she initially focused in on possible risks to the pipelines.

"I like the idea, it's creative," she said of Holley's suggestion. "And I would say that reusing assets is always good to think about. But when we change what kind of materials we move through a pipeline, we do need to be very cognizant about what that means for the integrity of the pipeline. What is appropriate for one substance may not be for another."

The high strength steel used to construct most pipelines for natural gas transport is designed to withstand the pressure of liquified gas, but does not have the additives to combat corrosion caused by long periods of disuse or river water with various contaminants and different electrochemical properties, Garcia explained.

Coal slurry lines, which are often made of concrete like Phoenix's canals, could potentially work better, though they don't span the distances the natural gas networks do. But remediation to ensure drinking water quality in that case, which she's never heard about anyone trying to do, could require purification treatment on both ends of the pipeline — to limit contaminants going in and to remove chemicals leached into water during transport at delivery.

At that point, like Roerink, Garcia concluded that the savings of recycling a pipeline network don't add up to a promising plan. Instead, she favors federal water rights buyout programs, which pay major users, often farmers, to retire their claims to water that, thanks to climate change, no longer exists except on paper.

This solution would help incentivize more efficient agricultural practices and urban conservation advances while resetting flow expectations to align with the reality currently delivered to us by our warmed and dried environments.

"We basically just have a supply and demand imbalance," Garcia said. "And we can do a lot of things to move water around in space and time, but we will have to address demand if we're going to ever stop that consistent juggling."

Joan Meiners is the climate news and storytelling reporter at The Arizona Republic and azcentral.com. Send tips or questions to joan.meiners@arizonarepublic.com or follow her work on Instagram at @joan_bikes_arizona.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Moving forests to save them: Here are the risks and rewards of assisted tree migration
item.description:
Climate change is shifting forest conditions faster than local tree species can adapt. Higher temperatures, more frequent droughts and less predictable winters can weaken species that have been established in a region for centuries. In light of this, one idea is gaining ground: assisted migration.

Assisted migration involves deliberately moving tree species or populations to regions where the future climate is expected to be more favourable. The aim is to anticipate changes rather than wait for forests to decline. While this approach may sound logical, it raises several important questions.

Helping forests adapt is a legitimate ambition, but ecosystems are complex, and any intervention has its limits. Five key issues help us better understand the potential of assisted migration and the precautions it requires.

As an assistant professor at the Eau Terre Environnement (ETE) Research Centre at the Institut national de la recherche scientifique (INRS), I have been working on the DREAM project, specifically on aspects related to carbon dynamics.

My co-author, Mariétou Diouf, is a specialist in tree ecophysiology, and Alison Munson is currently working on several assisted migration projects for trees in Québec.

The risk of invasion

The primary concern relates to the risk of ecological invasion. Moving a species outside its natural range can, in some cases, disrupt the balance of local ecosystems. Some newly introduced species can spread rapidly and take over at the expense of existing vegetation.

However, this does not happen systematically, since the establishment of a tree depends on numerous factors that change over time, including soil quality, the local climate, interactions with other living organisms and the specific characteristics of the species itself.

Studies have shown that some displaced species struggle to establish themselves or have lower survival rates in their new environment, while others benefit from favourable conditions that facilitate their establishment.

So far, most assisted migration projects have been experimental, which limits the observation of potential impacts in different contexts.

International studies have demonstrated that certain species introduced can permanently alter the biological properties of the soil and influence how other species establish themselves, sometimes several years after introduction. This possibility highlights the importance of proceeding with caution and closely monitoring transplant sites.

Surviving today, but withering tomorrow

Even when a tree successfully establishes itself, there is no guarantee that it will adapt to future conditions. The climate continues to change, sometimes more rapidly than anticipated.

Scientists use climate models to anticipate these changes and guide planting decisions. These tools are valuable, but they cannot predict the future with certainty. Some trees may find themselves ill-suited to new climate extremes, such as intense heatwaves or late frosts.

Researchers favour using a step-by-step approach to limit this risk. They test different populations of the same species, sourced from various climatic regions, and observe how they behave in new environments. Experimental programs in North America illustrate this cautious method, based on learning over time.

This is the case with the DREAM project, as well as initiatives such as the Assisted Migration Adaptation Trial and TransX, which study the survival and growth of trees relocated to different sites and climates. Despite these efforts, some uncertainty remains, particularly in fragmented landscapes where forests have fewer opportunities to adapt naturally.

Invisible effects on the soil

When we talk about assisted migration, we often think of the trees themselves, but the soil plays an equally important role. Roots interact with an underground world of bacteria, fungi and other microorganisms that are essential to forest health.

These organisms help trees absorb water and nutrients, and strengthen their resistance to environmental stresses. If a tree is moved to a site where these partners are absent or different, its growth may be hindered.

Conversely, the presence of certain species can permanently alter the soil and, as a result, shape future vegetation through what’s known as the ecological legacy effect.

These changes are often invisible in the short term, but can have long-term consequences for the entire ecosystem. To better understand these subtle yet crucial interactions, assisted migration projects incorporate soil analyses and preliminary trials.

Interactions with herbivores

Insects, pathogens, deer or rodents can significantly influence the survival and growth of young plants. Although assisted migration can help certain vulnerable wildlife species adapt to habitats disrupted by climate change, relocated trees still have to contend with local wildlife.

Some newly introduced species may be tasty to local herbivores, leading to significant damage. Other species, meanwhile, may be consumed less frequently, thereby altering existing food balances. In any case, these interactions play a key role in the success or failure of tree migration.

To address this, researchers are working with forest managers to test various solutions, such as temporary fencing, selecting more resilient seedlings and management practices that promote better coexistence with wildlife. These approaches show that assisted migration is not limited to climate, but involves the entire ecological network.

Logistical constraints, economic risks

Even when ecological aspects are taken into account, assisted migration poses practical challenges. Producing seedlings, transporting them, planting them and monitoring them represent a significant investment of time and resources.

On a large scale, these costs can become considerable, especially since forests are often fragmented by roads and agricultural or urban areas, complicating the planning and implementation of plantations.

Assisted migration also raises economic issues related to risk-taking. Introducing unfamiliar species creates new uncertainties that forest owners — especially private ones — must learn to manage. This raises questions about the allocation of investments and the role of public land in implementing these strategies.

Social and ethical questions then arise: Which species should be prioritized? Who decides? And how can climate adaptation, biodiversity and local forest uses be reconciled?

Guiding the forests of tomorrow

Assisted migration offers promising prospects for protecting forests from climate change. It is based on a simple idea: anticipating and acting rather than suffering the consequences.

However, challenges relating to invasions, long-term adaptation, interactions with soil and wildlife and practical constraints serve as a reminder that forest ecosystems are complex.

Ongoing research is helping to improve our understanding of these mechanisms and to provide a framework for best practices. By recognizing the limitations of assisted migration and integrating it into a broader forest management strategy, it becomes possible to use it as a complementary and flexible tool.

Helping forests adapt to climate change does not mean controlling everything but, rather, learning to intervene with discernment while respecting the diversity and dynamics of living systems.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: India's maternal health systems were designed for a climate that no longer exists. This ... - The Hindu
item.description:
India’s maternal health infrastructure has expanded significantly over the last two decades. More women are delivering in health facilities. Antenatal care coverage has improved. ASHA workers have become the backbone of community health outreach. These are important achievements.

The challenge is that many of these systems were designed for a climate that no longer exists.

A recent analysis by Climate Central found that India now experiences six additional “pregnancy heat-risk days” each year because of climate change. These are days when temperatures are high enough to increase the risk of adverse pregnancy outcomes. In Mumbai alone, climate change added 26 such days over a five-year period.

The consequences are serious. A major review published in Nature Medicine in 2024, covering 198 studies across 66 countries, found that heatwaves increase the risk of preterm birth by 26%. Research among outdoor workers in India has linked heat stress to a substantially higher risk of miscarriage. Heat exposure is also associated with low birth weight and stillbirth.

For India, these risks matter enormously. The country already carries one of the world’s largest burdens of preterm birth, with roughly one in six babies born before term. A preterm baby is far more likely to die in the first week of life than one born at full term. Progress made over decades in maternal and newborn health is now being tested by a changing climate.

The burden on frontline workers

The same lesson applies to frontline health workers. More than a million ASHAs work across India, often walking long distances in extreme heat to monitor pregnancies, support immunisation and deliver essential services. Yet they receive limited protection from the very climate risks they are helping communities navigate.

Our recent report points out that climate resilience is often carried on the shoulders of frontline workers long before it appears in policy. For maternal health, this means ASHAs and community health workers are increasingly responding to climate-related stresses - from heat exposure and water scarcity to food insecurity and disrupted access to care - without necessarily having the training, protocols, or institutional support to do so. The result is that resilience exists, but often in informal and under-recognised ways.

When heat becomes unbearable and visits are delayed or reduced, the effects ripple through the health system. Pregnancies go unmonitored. Health records develop gaps. These impacts rarely appear in climate statistics, but they are climate impacts nonetheless.

India does not lack programmes, plans, or institutions. What it often lacks is coordination between them. Climate and health continue to be governed through parallel systems, with heat treated as a disaster-management issue, climate change as an environmental issue, and maternal health as a health-sector issue. Pregnant women, however, experience all of these risks at once.

What needs to change

Heat Action Plans should explicitly identify pregnant women, newborns and frontline health workers as priority groups. Antenatal care programmes should incorporate heat-risk assessments, including questions about living conditions, work exposure and access to cooling. ASHAs need climate-responsive training and recognition of heat exposure as an occupational health issue. Health facilities require investments in cooling measures, whether through improved design, cool roofs, ventilation or reliable power. And India needs better data systems to track the relationship between extreme heat and maternal and neonatal outcomes.

None of these measures require building an entirely new system. They require updating the systems we already have.

A striking example comes from Ahmedabad. During the severe 2010 heatwave, doctors observed a sharp rise in newborns arriving with unexplained fevers. Investigations revealed that the maternity ward was located on the top floor of a building with a heat-absorbing roof. Simply relocating the ward to a cooler floor reduced heat-related admissions. Sometimes adaptation is not about sophisticated technology; it is about recognising risks that were previously invisible.

Adapting progress to a hotter future

India’s maternal health story over the past two decades has been one of steady progress. Climate change is now a stress test for that progress. The heat that women experience today is not the heat their mothers experienced during pregnancy.

And temperature alone does not determine vulnerability. What matters is everything around it. The women most exposed to climate-related health risks are often those already facing economic and social disadvantage. Vulnerability is not natural; it is shaped by housing, livelihoods, access to services and the strength of public systems.

The risks are changing, and our institutions must change with them. The encouraging news is that the foundations already exist. The question is whether we will adapt them quickly enough to protect the women and children whose health increasingly depends on it.

Doing so will require action from multiple actors. Policymakers must ensure that climate considerations are embedded within maternal and child health programmes, Heat Action Plans, and health-system planning. Funders and philanthropies must invest in the evidence, innovation, and frontline capacity needed to build climate-resilient health systems. Civil society organisations, many of which are already responding to climate-health challenges on the ground, need greater support to test, demonstrate, and scale solutions that reach vulnerable communities.

Climate change is no longer only an environmental challenge; it is a public health challenge. And if India’s progress in maternal and child health is to be sustained, health must move from the margins of climate action to its centre.

(Pranay Karuna is associate director & head of programmes, ClimateRISE, Dasra. pranay@dasra.org)

Published - July 05, 2026 07:18 pm IST
